text:
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known EGFR L858R-mutant right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma, stage IIIB at diagnosis, initially treated with osimertinib. Recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated enlargement of the right lower lobe primary mass, a new right adrenal nodule, new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and recurrent mediastinal adenopathy, raising concern for systemic progression after approximately 14 months of targeted therapy. MRI brain obtained urgently to evaluate for intracranial involvement.

TECHNIQUE: MRI of the brain was performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1 MPRAGE pre-contrast, axial T2 FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial gradient echo (GRE/SWI), axial T1 pre-contrast, and axial and coronal T1 post-contrast following intravenous administration of gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg. Multiplanar reformatted images reviewed.

COMPARISON: MRI brain with and without contrast performed at the time of initial staging evaluation (approximately 14 months prior): no intracranial metastases.

---

FINDINGS:

INTRACRANIAL METASTATIC LESIONS:
Three discrete enhancing parenchymal lesions are identified, all new since the prior brain MRI:

1. RIGHT CEREBELLAR HEMISPHERE: A well-circumscribed, avidly ring-enhancing lesion measuring approximately 1.1 cm in greatest axial dimension is identified in the right cerebellar hemisphere, lateral to the midline. The lesion demonstrates a hypointense core on T1 pre-contrast with thin, irregular peripheral enhancement post-gadolinium. On T2/FLAIR, there is a modest halo of surrounding hyperintense signal consistent with vasogenic edema extending into the adjacent cerebellar white matter. No evidence of internal hemorrhage on GRE/susceptibility-weighted imaging. No herniation or tonsillar descent. No significant mass effect on the fourth ventricle.

2. LEFT FRONTAL LOBE: A smaller enhancing lesion measuring approximately 0.8 cm is identified at the gray-white junction of the left frontal lobe, in the posterior aspect of the superior frontal gyrus. Post-gadolinium sequences demonstrate solid nodular enhancement. Mild surrounding T2/FLAIR signal change is present. No hemorrhagic component on susceptibility imaging. No cortical involvement or sulcal effacement.

3. RIGHT PARIETAL LOBE: A third lesion measuring approximately 0.6 cm is identified at the gray-white junction of the right parietal lobe, in the region of the postcentral gyrus. Solid nodular enhancement is noted on post-contrast sequences. Minimal associated T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. No hemorrhage. No significant mass effect.

All three lesions are located at or near the gray-white junction, a distribution highly characteristic of hematogenous metastatic spread. The enhancing morphology, clinical context, and pattern of involvement are most consistent with metastatic disease.

LEPTOMENINGEAL DISEASE: No sulcal, cisternal, or ependymal enhancement to suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis. The cranial nerves are unremarkable bilaterally.

VENTRICLES AND CISTERNS: The ventricular system is normal in caliber. There is no obstructive hydrocephalus. Basal cisterns are patent.

BRAIN PARENCHYMA: No territorial infarct. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemic injury. White matter signal changes are mild and nonspecific, age-appropriate. Cortical thickness and gyral pattern are preserved. No evidence of encephalomalacia.

EXTRA-AXIAL SPACES: No subdural or epidural collection. Calvarium and skull base are intact without suspicious osseous lesion. Visualized paranasal sinuses show trace mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus, likely incidental.

PITUITARY AND POSTERIOR FOSSA: Pituitary gland appears normal without a discrete adenoma. No acoustic schwannoma. Craniocervical junction is unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Three new enhancing parenchymal lesions compared to prior MRI brain obtained approximately 14 months ago, at the time of initial staging. The lesions are located in the right cerebellar hemisphere (1.1 cm, ring-enhancing with surrounding edema), left frontal lobe (0.8 cm, solid enhancement), and right parietal lobe (0.6 cm, solid enhancement). The pattern, distribution at the gray-white junction, and clinical context are consistent with intracranial metastases from the patient's known lung adenocarcinoma.

2. No leptomeningeal enhancement.

3. No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage.

4. No herniation or hydrocephalus. Mass effect from the cerebellar lesion is limited and does not appear to threaten the fourth ventricle or cerebellar tonsils at this time.

Correlation with systemic restaging findings and multidisciplinary neurosurgical and radiation oncology input is recommended for treatment planning.
